curated.name: Papakōlea Beach
curated.description: Unlike cinder cones, tuff rings consist mostly of volcanic ash produced by violent interactions of magma with groundwater (Diamond Head, on the Island of Oahu, is another example of a tuff ring).
curated.story: Papakolea is one of only four green-sand beaches on Earth. The color comes from olivine crystals — a silicate of iron and magnesium — eroded out of the nearby Puu Mahana cinder cone. Olivine is denser than the surrounding basaltic ash, so it accumulates here instead of being washed out to sea.

curated.name: Punalu'u Black Sand Beach
curated.story: Punalu'u's jet-black sand is ground basalt, formed when molten lava entering the Pacific shattered into fine fragments through thermal shock. Unlike the quartz-rich beaches of the mainland, none of this sand comes from rivers — it is all freshly minted volcanic rock.

curated.name: Reynisfjara
curated.story: Reynisfjara's pitch-black sand is fragmented basalt from the Katla volcanic system, deposited by the turbulent North Atlantic. The beach is backed by columnar basalt cliffs where rapid cooling of thick lava produced regular hexagonal joints.

curated.name: Glass Beach
curated.story: Decades of municipal dumping at Fort Bragg produced the world's most famous glass beach. Relentless Pacific surf has tumbled the broken bottles into rounded, frosted pebbles of green, brown, white, and rare blue. The glass is gradually being worn away and the beach is no longer self-replenishing.

curated.name: Whitehaven Beach
curated.description: The beach is seven kilometres in length and is famous for its white silica sands and turquoise water.
curated.story: Whitehaven's sand is 98-99% pure silica — unusually fine quartz that reflects sunlight rather than absorbing it, keeping the sand cool in tropical heat. Geologists believe the deposit was transported by ancient currents from a source still not conclusively identified.

curated.name: Hyams Beach
curated.story: Hyams Beach held a Guinness World Record for the whitest sand. The grains are high-purity quartz with minimal iron-oxide staining — a combination rarely found outside Australia's east coast, where the right bedrock, climate, and littoral transport converge.

curated.name: Kaihalulu Beach
curated.story: Kaihalulu's red sand comes from iron-rich cinders eroded from the crumbling cliff of Ka'uiki Head. The vivid brick-red color reflects oxidation of iron in the volcanic ash — essentially rust, frozen into the rock millennia ago and now returning to the sea.

curated.name: Navagio
curated.story: Navagio's sand is pale limestone gravel and fine carbonate, the weathered remains of the Triassic limestone cliffs that enclose the cove. The sand is noticeably brighter than surrounding beaches because it contains almost no silicate minerals — only dissolved and re-precipitated calcium carbonate.

curated.name: Playa de Gulpiyuri
curated.story: Gulpiyuri sits 100 metres inland, in a sinkhole fed by seawater through a karst tunnel. Its sand is pure shell carbonate, washed in with each tide through the limestone aquifer — a rare inland beach that breathes with the Cantabrian Sea.

curated.name: Shell Beach
curated.story: Guyana's Shell Beach is named not for decorative shells but for the immense bank of cockle and clam fragments that makes up its substrate. There is effectively no mineral sand — just millennia of molluscan carbonate, replenished by the Amazon's nutrient plume and deposited by coastal drift.

curated.name: Porto Ferro
curated.story: Porto Ferro's orange-red sand is a mix of iron-oxide-rich quartz grains weathered from the surrounding Sardinian sandstone, combined with biogenic shell fragments. The combination produces a distinct rust tone that deepens when the sand is wet.

curated.name: Ir-Ramla t' Għajn Tuffieħa
curated.story: Malta's Ramla Bay has brick-red sand from weathered iron-rich sandstone of the Upper Coralline Limestone cap. It is the most-pigmented sand on the Maltese islands and exists only because the red-bed source rocks outcrop directly above this single bay.

curated.name: Cox's Bazar Beach
curated.story: At 120 km, Cox's Bazar is often claimed as the longest natural sand beach on Earth. The sand is fine quartz sourced from the Ganges-Brahmaputra sediment system — the same Himalayan-erosion feedstock that builds most of Bangladesh's coastline.

curated.name: One Cable Beach
curated.story: Bahamian Cable Beach is almost entirely oolitic aragonite — tiny concentric spheres of calcium carbonate that precipitate directly from the warm, supersaturated Bahama Banks seawater. Walking on oolite feels unusually smooth because each grain is nearly a perfect sphere.

curated.name: Te Puia / Hot Water Beach
curated.story: Hot Water Beach's sand is iron-rich titanomagnetite eroded from Coromandel's andesitic volcanoes. Geothermal water seeps up through the black sand at low tide, letting visitors dig private hot pools — a rare intersection of sand geology and active geothermal plumbing.

curated.name: Muriwai Beach
curated.story: Muriwai's black sand is titanomagnetite derived from the Taranaki volcanic province. The iron content is high enough that a magnet dragged through the sand comes out coated — and historically the beach has been mined for its iron-oxide content.

curated.name: Durdle Door
curated.story: Durdle Door sits at the foot of the Jurassic Coast's Portland limestone arch. The beach pebbles are a cross-section of 150 million years of geology: Jurassic limestone, Wealden sandstone, and flint chalk that has weathered out of the Cretaceous cliffs overhead.

curated.name: ETRETAT-PLAGE
curated.story: Etretat's beach is pure white chalk-flint shingle. There is almost no sand at all — the beach is rounded pebbles of flint nodules eroded from the Cretaceous chalk cliffs, whose carbonate matrix dissolves in seawater leaving only the silica-rich flint behind.

curated.name: Matira Beach
curated.story: Matira's fine white sand is pulverised coral and foraminifera from the surrounding barrier reef — nearly pure calcium carbonate. Unlike quartz-beach sands, carbonate sand is slightly alkaline and dissolves over geologic time, making it a finite resource in reef systems.

curated.name: Copacabana
curated.story: Copacabana's golden quartz sand was trucked in and renourished in the 1970s as part of the beach's expansion to nearly 100 metres width. The native sand — finer, slightly greyer — still outcrops at the eastern end near the Forte de Copacabana, where the original shoreline is preserved.
